docs.title: My Homelab
docs.text: Welcome to my public homelab documentation. This site collects notes, topology diagrams, deployments, and recipes I use to build and operate my home lab.

docs.title: Quick Links
docs.text:
• Network Topology — diagrams and device roles.
  • Documentation Index — this page.

docs.title: About
docs.text: This repository documents my homelab projects: network design, servers, containers, automation, backups, and monitoring. Content is organized into folders by topic.

docs.title: How to Contribute / Use
docs.text:
• Browse the folders in the left-hand navigation.
  • Open pages for commands, diagrams, and configuration examples.
  • If you find errors or have suggestions, open a GitHub issue or PR.

docs.title: Windows LAB Environment Overview
docs.text: This documentation describes a lab-scale Windows infrastructure deployed on Hyper-V. It is designed to demonstrate network segmentation, firewall management, and Active Directory domain services in a professional lab environment.

docs.title: Objectives
docs.text:
• Build a Hyper-V lab with separate WAN and production network segments.
  • Deploy OPNsense as the perimeter firewall and gateway.
  • Deploy a Windows Server Domain Controller with Active Directory, DNS, and DHCP.

docs.title: Lab scope
docs.text:
• Hyper-V virtual switch configuration
  • OPNsense firewall installation and LAN/WAN setup
  • Windows Server domain controller installation, promotion, and service configuration

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
• Hyper-V Environment Setup
  • Installing OPNsense
  • Installing Domain Controller

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This lab is intended to demonstrate foundational Windows infrastructure skills, including network segmentation, secure firewall placement, and centralized identity and service management.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Hyper-V lab environment is built using two virtual switches to separate the internet-facing network from the internal production network. This structure supports a secure firewall perimeter and a dedicated domain controller network.

docs.title: Virtual switches
docs.text:
• vSwitch-WAN - External network connected to the host's physical NIC. This switch carries WAN traffic for the firewall.
  • vSwitch-Prod - Private internal network for lab infrastructure, including the domain controller and internal services.

docs.title: Recommended configuration
docs.text:
• Create the external switch with the host adapter connected to your physical network.
  • Create the private switch as an isolated internal network with no direct host access.
  • Attach the OPNsense VM with two NICs: one on vSwitch-WAN and one on vSwitch-Prod.
  • Attach the Windows Server VM to vSwitch-Prod only.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Use descriptive switch names to keep the environment easy to maintain.
  • Keep the lab network isolated from production or home networks when possible.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This server is configured as a Windows Server domain controller for the lab environment. It provides Active Directory Domain Services, DNS, and DHCP for the internal production network.

docs.title: Lab resources
docs.text:
• CPU: 2 cores
  • RAM: 4 GB
  • Storage: 60 GB
  • Network adapter: vSwitch-Prod
  • Secure Boot: Enabled
  • TPM: Enabled

docs.title: Installation steps
docs.text:
1. Boot the VM from the Windows Server evaluation ISO.
  2. Install the operating system to the virtual hard disk.
  3. Configure the local Administrator password.
  4. Set the server hostname to LAB-DC01.
  5. Configure a static network address:
  6. IP address: 10.0.0.10
  7. Subnet mask: 255.255.255.0
  8. Default gateway: 10.0.0.1
  9. Preferred DNS server: 10.0.0.10

docs.title: Role and feature installation
docs.text:
1. Install the following Windows Server roles and features:
  2. Active Directory Domain Services
  3. DNS Server
  4. DHCP Server
  5. Reboot the server after the installation completes.
  6. Promote the server to a domain controller:
  7. Domain name: fakeprod.local
  8. Forest and domain functional level: Windows Server 2025

docs.title: DNS and DHCP configuration
docs.text:
• Verify the DNS forward lookup zone for fakeprod.local was created automatically.
  • Create a reverse lookup zone for the 10.0.0.0/24 network.
  • Configure a DHCP scope for the internal network:
  • Scope name: Prod
  • Address range: 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.254
  • Subnet mask: 255.255.255.0
  • Exclusions: 10.0.0.1 through 10.0.0.20

docs.title: Administrative user
docs.text:
• Create a domain user named Bryce.
  • Add the user to the following groups:
  • Administrators
  • Domain Admins
  • Enterprise Admins

docs.title: Post-installation notes
docs.text:
• Disable DHCP on OPNsense once the domain controller is responsible for the internal DHCP scope.
  • Validate DNS resolution and Active Directory replication.
  • Confirm the domain controller can service internal clients on the production network.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: OPNsense is deployed as the perimeter firewall and gateway for the lab. It provides WAN connectivity, LAN routing, and DHCP for the internal network until the domain controller takes over DHCP services.

docs.title: Lab resources
docs.text:
• CPU: 1 core
  • RAM: 3 GB
  • Storage: 40 GB
  • Network adapters:
  • NIC1: vSwitch-WAN
  • NIC2: vSwitch-Prod
  • Secure Boot: Disabled
  • TPM: Enabled

docs.title: Installation steps
docs.text:
1. Boot the VM from the OPNsense ISO image.
  2. Assign the network interfaces manually when prompted.
  3. Log in to the installer using the default credentials:
  4. username: installer
  5. password: opnsense
  6. Complete the installation and set a secure root password.
  7. Configure the interface addresses:
  8. WAN: DHCP
  9. LAN: 10.0.0.1/24

docs.title: Post-install configuration
docs.text:
• Enable the LAN DHCP server for the 10.0.0.0/24 network during initial setup.
  • Reserve the production DHCP range or configure exclusions for infrastructure addresses.
  • Once the Windows domain controller is configured, disable OPNsense DHCP on the LAN to avoid conflicts.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Use the OPNsense web interface for firewall rule management and service monitoring.
  • Confirm WAN connectivity before promoting internal services.

docs.title: This is an example network topology
docs.text: Examples

docs.title: Linksys WHW01-V1 Mesh - OpenWrt Upgrade and Mesh Setup
Executive Summary
docs.text: This project documents reclaiming three Linksys WHW01-V1 (Velop Dual-Band) access points by installing OpenWrt 25.12.5 and configuring an 802.11s encrypted mesh. The goal of this project is to improve the overall security of EOL consumer hardware by transitioning to open source software, as well as adding enhanced security features such as WPA3, VLAN segmentation and granular firewall policies.

docs.title: Hardware and Software Specifications
docs.text:
• Devices: 3x Linksys WHW01-V1 (Velo Dual-Band)
  • Stock Firmware: Linksys factory image (1.1.13.202617)
  • Target Firmware: OpenWrt 25.12.5 (Latest release at time of install)

docs.title: Current Software Limitations (Stock)
docs.text:
• Limited to WPA2
  • WPS enabled by default
  • UPnP enabled by default
  • Weak and limited parental controls and firewall features
  • Hardware is End-of-Life (EOL) with no ongoing vendor support or vulnerability patching

docs.title: Implementation Procedure
docs.text: [!WARNING] "Mesh Configuration Consistency" The following wireless and mesh configurations must be applied identically across all nodes for the 802.11s mesh and client roaming to function properly.

docs.title: Installing OpenWrt
docs.text:
• Reset to factory defaults (The reset password feature can also be utilized)
  • Download latest supported version of OpenWrt for the WHW01-V1 from the official site
  • In the Linksys GUI, navigate to "connectivity" settings and click "CA" at the bottom of the page. (This will give you the ability to select a manual update file)
  • Upload the OpenWrt image
  • After the system updates, access the OpenWrt GUI through 192.168.1.1
  • Login to OpenWrt (username = root, password = blank)

### OpenWrt Interface Configurations

  • Navigate to Network -> Interfaces
    • Create a new interface called "wan" and assign it to "ETH2" (ETH2 is the top ethernet port)
    • Edit the "lan" interface
      • Set a static IPv4 address for each node (e.g., 10.0.0.1/24 for node-1, 10.0.0.2/24 for node-2, 10.0.0.3/24 for node-3)
      • Parent node: Set IPv4 (e.g., 10.0.0.1/24) and Enable DHCP,
      • Child nodes: Set IPv4 (e.g., 10.0.0.2/24), set the Gateway and DNS to parent node IP, and Disable DHCP
  • Navigate to Network -> Devices
  • Configure "br-lan" and remove "ETH2" from the bridge ports

[!note] The WAN connection must now be connected to "ETH2".

  • Navigate to System -> Software
  • Select Actions: Update lists...
  • Remove "wpad-basic-mbedtls" (This package must be removed as it lacks 802.11s mesh support)

  • Install "wpad-mesh-openssl"

  • Navigate to System -> System

  • Set unique hostnames for each node (e.g., node-1, node-2, node-3)

  • Set the local timezone

  • Reboot the devices

docs.title: Configuring the Wireless Mesh
docs.text:
• Navigate to Network -> Wireless
  • Add a new 5GHz interface
  • Set Operating frequency. (Both the channel and width must be statically set)
  • Change mode to "802.11s"
  • Set Mesh Id (e.g., whw01_mesh)
  • Set Network to "lan"
  • Set Encryption to "WPA3-SAE" and configure a secure Key

Validation: At this stage, peer nodes should be visible under the "Associated Stations" table in the wireless overview. If missing, verify all mesh settings are identical and radios are enabled.

docs.title: Configuring Wireless Access Points
docs.text:
Setting up the client-facing wireless networks follows a similar procedure. All ESSID and security settings must be identical across nodes to facilitate device roaming.

  • Under the 2.4Ghz and 5Ghz radios, click Add:
  • Channel/Width: Select statically (Note: 5GHz AP must be on the same channel as the mesh due to the lack of a dedicated backhaul radio)
  • Mode: Access Point
  • ESSID: Wirless_Name (e.g., OpenWrt)
  • Network: lan
  • Encryption: WPA3-SAE
  • Key: [WiFi-Password]
  • 802.11r Fast Transition: Enabled

docs.title: Performance Analysis
docs.text: During testing the max throughput observed was ~250Mbps. This represents a best case scenario as testing was done with only one device connected to the network. As devices on the network increases, the shared 5GHz radio will become overwhelmed and be a bottleneck. Latency and throughput would be affected as a result.

docs.title: Operational Drawbacks
docs.text:
1. Decentralized Management: Every configuration change must be done to all nodes manually and identically. Any slight variation in network or wireless settings will break the mesh topology. The addition of a centralized controller or automation system would greatly increase the usability of the system.

  2. Channel Constraints/Interference: The Linksys WHW01-V1 is only a dual band system. All 5Ghz traffic (mesh backhaul and access) must use the same channel. Due to OpenWrt 802.11s requiring statically assigned channels rather than dynamic selection, this setup is susceptive to co-channel interference especially in congested wireless environments.

  3. Half-Duplex Operation: The mesh backhaul and client AP both operate over the singular 5Ghz radio. To support this, the radio must alternate between receiving data from a client and transmitting data to the parent node. This configuration halves the available throughput for mesh connected devices.

docs.title: Future Configurations
docs.text:
1. VLANs: Enabling 802.1Q VLANs instead of having a flat layer-2 network would greatly improve the security by isolating trusted devices from untrusted devices such as IoT.

  2. Advanced Firewall Policies: While the default NAT firewall rules in OpenWrt allow for network connectivity, creating granular rules based on VLAN, device type, etc would continue to restrict exposure to critical home lab systems.

  3. Centralized Management: Using automation such as Ansible would allow for greater ease of use for this mesh setup.

  4. VPNs: Integrating WireGuard or OpenVPN would allow for secure out-of-band management and remote access to my internal resources.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: Replacing the stock Linksys firmware with OpenWrt revived unsupported hardware, introduced modern WPA3 encryption, enabled 802.11s encrypted mesh networking, and unlocked enterprise routing capabilities. For home‑lab and portfolio purposes, this demonstrates practical hardware reclamation and the fundamentals of a secure wireless mesh topology.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• OpenWrt device page: https://openwrt.org/toh/linksys/whw01_v1
  • Linksys initial setup (vendor): https://support.linksys.com/kb/article/58-en/
